text: The surgeon begins by mobilizing the liver and exposing the anterior wall of the choledochal cyst, carefully dissecting it. The dissection proceeds laterally, separating the cyst from surrounding structures. The upper neck of the cyst is identified and bluntly dissected, separating it from the portal vein and hepatic artery. The upper neck is then secured with a vessel loop. The cyst is circumferentially dissected down towards the pancreas, revealing the lower neck in the extra-pancreatic portion of the common bile duct. The cyst is partially opened, exposing biliary sludge and a plastic stent, which is removed.

The surgeon transfixes the lower neck of the cyst with a poly-dianone thread reinforced with two hemologs, creating a distal section between them. Anterior traction is applied to expose the back wall of the cyst, which is carefully dissected despite adhesions from previous cholecystectomy and local inflammation. The proximal resection is performed one centimeter below the hepatic duct confluence, sharply transecting the cyst. The entire cyst is removed in a plastic bag, with spontaneous outflow of biliary stones from the proximal biliary tree visible. Colegoclisis is then performed.

The surgeon identifies the division point of the proximal jejunum, located sixty centimeters from the angle of traits, and divides the small bowel using a laparoscopic stapler. An ultrasonic device is employed to divide the mesentery towards the base and create an enterotomy on the anti-mesenteric side of the root limb, four centimeters from the stapled end. An end-to-side anastomosis is performed between the duct and the jejunum using full thickness bites in a single layer, interrupted fashion with a non-absorbable monofilament suture.

The surgeon creates a suture row by stitching from inside-out on the bowel and outside-in on the bile duct. The enterotomy is visibly smaller than the bile duct transection to prevent stretching during suturing. The completed suture is secured to surrounding structures to minimize tension.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy between the alimentary and biliary limb, creating small adjacent enterotomies in each limb. A vascular staple load is used to close the common enterostomy, excluding the mesentery from the suture lines. The mesenteric defect is closed with interwrapped non-absorbable suture, and a subhepatic drainage is placed near the anastomosis.
